title: Tree Traversals: Pre-order, In-order, Post-order
description: Formally naming the printInOrder pattern from Binary Search Trees, and its two siblings -- three ways to visit every node, differing only in WHEN you process the current node.
objectives: Implement pre-order, in-order, and post-order traversal recursively
Understand each traversal's visiting order and a use case for each
Recognize these as depth-first traversals (previewed for the next lesson)

sections.text: In Binary Search Trees, printInOrder visited left, then the node, then right. That's ONE of three classic traversal orders -- all three visit every node exactly once, differing only in WHEN the current node is processed relative to its children.

We'll trace all three traversals against this SAME tree throughout this lesson:
        1
       / \
      2   3
     / \
    4   5
Pre-order visits the node BEFORE its children -- useful for creating a COPY of a tree, since you write down the parent before its children exist in the copy.

This is the EXACT function that was called printInOrder in Binary Search Trees, now given its formal name. Recall: for a BST specifically, in-order traversal always visits values in ascending sorted order -- that guarantee does NOT hold for a plain (non-BST) binary tree, like the one used in this lesson.

Post-order visits the node AFTER both its children -- useful for DELETING a tree safely (delete children before the parent that references them) or computing something that depends on children's results first, like the height calculation from Binary Trees, which is itself a post-order-shaped computation.

sections.title: The three orders, side by side
rows: Getting sorted output from a BST
Deleting a tree, or computations needing children's results first

sections.text: All three traversals dive as DEEP as possible down one branch before backtracking to explore another -- this is Depth-First Search (DFS), previewed here and covered as its own dedicated lesson, contrasted with Breadth-First Search (BFS / Level Order), covered right after.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Confusing the three traversal orders -- the only difference is WHERE the node's own processing line sits relative to the two recursive calls.
Assuming in-order traversal gives sorted output for ANY binary tree -- that guarantee only holds specifically for a Binary Search Tree.
Forgetting the base case (node == null) -- without it, every traversal crashes with a NullPointerException at the tree's edges.
Using the wrong traversal for a task -- e.g. using pre-order when a computation genuinely needs children's results first (post-order's job).

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the difference between pre-order, in-order, and post-order traversal?
questions.answer: They differ only in when the current node is processed relative to its children: pre-order processes it first (node, left, right), in-order processes it between its children (left, node, right), and post-order processes it last (left, right, node).

questions.question: Which traversal gives sorted output, and under what condition?
questions.answer: In-order traversal, but only when the tree is specifically a Binary Search Tree -- a plain binary tree with no BST ordering gives no such guarantee.

questions.question: Why is post-order traversal useful for deleting a tree?
questions.answer: It processes both children before the parent node itself, ensuring a node's children are fully handled (e.g. deleted) before the node that references them is removed.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Pre-order, in-order, and post-order all visit every node in a tree exactly once, differing only in when the current node is processed relative to recursing into its left and right children: pre-order processes the node first, in-order processes it between the two recursive calls, and post-order processes it last. In-order traversal specifically produces sorted output when the tree is a Binary Search Tree. Post-order suits tasks needing children's results first, like safe tree deletion or height computation. All three are forms of Depth-First Search, diving fully down one branch before backtracking -- contrasted with Breadth-First (Level Order) traversal, covered next.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Pre = node comes BEFORE its kids (prefix). In = node sits IN BETWEEN its kids (infix). Post = node comes AFTER its kids (postfix). Same recursion, node's print line just moves position.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What's the key difference between pre-order, in-order, and post-order?
For which kind of tree does in-order traversal give sorted output?
Why is post-order suited to safely deleting a tree?
What broader category of traversal do all three belong to?
